content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660           322-5901           222-6441           322-6444


July 26, 1979


Mr. John Doyle
City Councilman
Box 1736
Palm Springs, CA 92262

Dear Mr. Doyle:

Thank you for your letters of May 22, 1979, and June 1,
1979, requesting advice with respect to the disqualification
provisions of the Political Reform Act. I hope the following
advice provided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b)
is helpful.

The facts as I understand them are as follows. You and
a partner own a commercial property on Palm Canyon Drive in
Palm Springs. The property is in the central business dis-
trict and is currently leased to another person who operates a
retail store on the premises. Palm Canyon Drive is the primary
business street in the downtown area. The street has three
one-way southbound traffic lanes and two parking lanes. In
the immediate three or four block area of the central business
district, there is growing traffic congestion. There is con-
cern that within the next several years, it will be necessary
to take some steps to ease the traffic congestion. The City
Council directed city staff to conduct a traffic circulation
study of the entire community but with specific emphasis on
the circulation problems in downtown Palm Springs. Staff has
developed two alternative programs for Council consideration.
One program would require removal of the two parking lanes
from Palm Canyon Drive. This would provide the necessary street
width to add one additional traffic lane making a total of
four lanes southbound. A second alternative is to make further
improvements on Indian Avenue, a parallel street one block
west of Palm Canyon Drive in order to provide for the additional
traffic capacity on that street. Some people in the community
including some who serve on advisory boards and commissions
and some who are members of the Chamber of Commerce are opposed

Mr. John Doyle
July 26, 1979                                                Page Two


to the removal of parking from Palm Canyon Drive.  They believe
that the proposed changes to Palm Canyon Drive would be detri-
mental to businesses in the downtown area because customer
parking would be eliminated and because conversion of Palm
Canyon Drive to a four lane through street would reduce the
attractiveness of the area for commercial use.  It was because
of this opposition that the staff proposed traffic improvements
on Indian Avenue as an alternative to be considered by the
Council.

     You have asked whether you must disqualify yourself from
participating in the consideration of these proposals regarding
traffic in the downtown area because of your interest in real
property on Palm Canyon Drive.

     Government Code Section 87100 provides:1/

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

     Section 87103 provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:

          (b)  Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000). . . .

     It is my understanding that you have a greater than 10%
interest in the partnership which owns the commercial property
on Palm Canyon Drive and that your pro rata share of the property
is greater than $1,000.  Accordingly, you have an interest in
real property within the meaning of the Political Reform Act and
must disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which
foreseeably may materially affect the property in a manner
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.


_________________________

     1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Mr. John Doyle
July 26, 1979                                                    Page Three


     The Commission has determined that it is foreseeable that a
governmental decision will have a material financial effect on
an interest in real property when there is a substantial likeli-
hood that the interest will be significantly affected by the
decision. In this case, we believe that it is foreseeable that
the adoption of the plan to remove parking from Palm Canyon
Drive and convert it to a four lane through street will materially
affect the value of your interest in real property by reducing
its attractiveness for commercial uses. This conclusion is
buttressed by the beliefs of the Chamber of Commerce and many
people from the business community that the Palm Canyon Drive
plan would be detrimental to the business of the community and
that it would ruin the character of the downtown business commun-
ity. Accordingly, you must disqualify yourself from participating
in any decisions concerning the potential plans for Palm Canyon
Drive unless your interest will be affected in the same manner
as the public generally.

     The effects of a decision on the official's interest will
be indistinguishable from its effects on the public generally
if the effects of the decision on the official are substantially
similar to those on the public generally or a significant segment
thereof. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. In opinions
requested by William Owen, 2 FPPC Opinions 77 (No. 76-005)
and P. MacKenzie Brown, 4 FPPC Opinions 19 (No. 77-024), the
Commission stated that a city's business community constituted
a significant segment of the public. Thus, an official need
not disqualify himself from a decision which will affect his
interests in substantially the same manner as the rest of the
business community. However, in your case, much of the Palm Springs
business community is located away from the Palm Canyon Drive
area. Therefore, many businesses will not be affected in the
same manner as your interest.2/ For these reasons, we conclude
that the decision regarding Palm Canyon Drive will not affect
your interests in substantially the same manner as that of the
public generally or a significant segment thereof. Accordingly,
you must disqualify yourself from making, participating in the
making of or using your official position to influence the making
of a governmental decision to remove parking from and create a
fourth traffic lane on Palm Canyon Drive.


     2/ Assuming, however, that the entire business community
were to be affected similarly, you would still be precluded from
voting as the Owen opinion indicates that commercial lessors are
not a significant segment of the public.

Mr. John Doyle
July 26, 1979                                                    Page Four

     In addition, since the proposal concerning Indian Avenue is
an alternate to the Palm Canyon Drive proposal, you are also
prohibited from making or participating in any decision modifying
the traffic pattern on Indian Avenue. If you have any questions,
please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:ma

City of Palm Springs
CALIFORNIA

Box 1786, 92262                                Tel. 714/327-1411

June 1, 1979

Mr. Ted Primm
Fair Political Practises Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95814

ATTN:  Conflict of Interest Division

Dear Mr. Primm:

On May 22nd, I communicated with you, posing a question
about whether I should vote on an issue involving traffic
circulation in the City of Palm Springs.

On the last page of that letter, there is some erroneous
information and prior to coming to some conclusion in your
response to me, I wanted to be sure that you had the most
recent information available.

The area of concern is that the retail business community
in Palm Springs is located in the downtown area as well as
in several shopping centers. A recent detailed investigation
at City Hall has indicated that approximately 9% of
the business license revenue of the city is derived in the
downtown business community. Approximately 7% of the total
number of business licenses issued is located in this area.

In my previous correspondence, I had indicated this figure
to be 30%. In reality, I suspect that about 30% of the total
retail sales may come from this area though that is a pure
educated guess and no one has done any research through the
sales tax division of the state to determine if, in fact,
that is the case. Specifically, the only information we can
give regarding the size of the business community in down-
town Palm Springs are the 9% and 7% figures set forth above.

I hope that this will clarify matters.

Sincerely,

JOHN DOYLE
Councilman

JD/mm

City of Palm Springs
CALIFORNIA
Box 1786, 22262                                                Tel. 714/327-1441

May 22, 1979

Mr. Thomas K. Huston
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

ATTN: Conflict of Interest Division

Dear Mr. Huston:

I am a Councilmember in the City of Palm Springs and at
the present time, there is pending before the City Council, an action involving a decision with respect to traffic circulation in the downtown business district of the community. I am a property owner on Palm Canyon Drive,
which is one of the streets that could be affected by the
Council's final decision regarding a long term circulation program for the City. The question is, because I
am a property owner, should I not participate. I have
been advised by some that I should and by others that I
should not.

Those who say that I should not participate, indicated
that my property could be affected by the ultimate decision with respect to the final decision made by the Council. Those who say that I should participate, contend
that as a commercial property owner in the City, I would
not be affected any more or any less than any other commercial property owner that is located on Palm Canyon
Drive in the downtown business area.

Because of these conflicting opinions, I would appreciate
guidance and direction from the Fair Political Practices
Commission.

The factual information in the case is as follows:

Palm Canyon Drive is the main business street in the downtown area. It is one way, carrying three lanes of southbound traffic and two parking lanes. In the immediate

CITY OF PALM SPRINGS

page two

Mr. Thomas K. Huston


three or four block area of the main business district,
there is growing traffic congestion. There is concern
that within the next several years, it will be neces-
sary to take some steps to ease the traffic congestion
problems.

City Council directed City staff to conduct a traffic
circulation study of the entire community but with spe-
cific emphasis on the circulation problems in downtown
Palm Springs. Staff has developed two alternative pro-
grams for Council consideration. One program would have
the two parking lanes removed from Palm Canyon Drive and
thus providing the necessary street width to add one ad-
ditional lane, making a total of four lanes southbound.

A second alternative would be to make further improve-
ments on Indian Avenue, a parallel street one block to
the west and provide for the additional traffic carrying
capacity on that street.

There are those in the community who serve on Advisory
Boards and Commissions and are members of the Chamber of
Commerce who are opposed to the removal of parking from
Palm Canyon Drive in an effort to increase the traffic
carrying capacity of the street. It is felt that this
would be detrimental to the business of the community and
that it would also ruin the character of the downtown
business area by making it a main thoroughfare rather
than a street running through a sizable commercial dis-
trict.

Because of this expressed opposition, the alternative of
making traffic improvements on Indian Avenue was made by
staff as something that could be considered.

I and another person have, for approximately ten years, owned
commercial property, including a store building, on Palm
Canyon Drive. Until the present time, we have operated a
retail store, although effective July 1, 1979, my partner and
I will have liquidated our business and leased the property to
another party for a retail operation.

The retail business community in Palm Springs is located in
the downtown area as well as in several shopping centers.

CITY OF PALM SPRINGS

page two

Mr. Thomas Huston


A recent check at City Hall indicated that approximately
30% of the business license revenue of the city is derived in the downtown business community. Although I
must admit that this information constitutes business
license fees from industrial, retail and all other forms
of businesses within the community.

As noted above, I would appreciate some guidance on which
way to proceed. I am sure that there will be additional
information you will need to render some advice. If so,
you can reach me at 180 N. Palm Canyon Drive (714-325-2024)
which is the store number and 2462 Via Sonoma #D (714-327-3964) which is my home number.

Many thanks,

Sincerely,

[signature]
JOHN DOYLE
Councilman


JD/mm

sections.question: I am a Councilmember in the City of Palm Springs and at the present time, there is pending before the City Council, an action involving a decision with respect to traffic circulation in the downtown business district of the community. I am a property owner on Palm Canyon Drive, which is one of the streets that could be affected by the Council's final decision regarding a long term circulation program for the City. The question is, because I am a property owner, should I not participate.
sections.question_synthetic: Should a city councilmember recuse themselves from voting on a traffic circulation decision that could affect their commercial property on Palm Canyon Drive in downtown Palm Springs?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two letters: the initial May 22, 1979 request and a June 1, 1979 follow-up letter correcting factual information (business license revenue percentages). This is an incoming request document, not an FPPC response letter. No FPPC conclusion/advice is included in this document.

I am a property owner on Palm Canyon Drive, which is one of the streets that could be affected by the Council's final decision regarding a long term circulation program for the City. The question is, because I am a property owner, should I not participate.

embedding.summary: A Palm Springs city councilmember seeks FPPC guidance on whether to recuse himself from voting on a traffic circulation proposal that could affect his commercial property on Palm Canyon Drive, with conflicting advice received from others in the community.
